name: Estonia
summary: A highly digital, low-tax, safe little tech state (Bolt, Wise, Skype heritage) with cheap study, a practical 9-month graduate job-search permit and a strong passport — but a poor fit for this couple's end-goal: Estonia does NOT permit dual citizenship for naturalised citizens, so a naturalising Bangladeshi must renounce BD nationality (blocker), citizenship takes 8 years with a B1 Estonian (Finno-Ugric) exam, and the Muslim/Bangladeshi community is negligible (~0.7%, no purpose-built mosque). Derived overall sits below the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: Study in Estonia — official national portal
Police and Border Guard Board — Estonian citizenship (official)

job-market.summary: A vibrant, English-friendly startup scene for its size (Bolt, Wise) with decent salaries against low costs, but the market is essentially Tallinn-only and small.

pros.text: Punches above its weight in tech (Bolt, Wise, Skype heritage); software-engineer pay in Tallinn runs ~€48k–77k and AI roles ~€40k–72k, strong against low living costs.
Highly digital and English-friendly workplaces, and recent graduates are exempt from the standard foreign-worker salary threshold.

cons.text: The market is essentially Tallinn-only and small, limiting depth for two simultaneous tech earners.
Estonian helps for many non-startup roles and public-facing work.

links.title: Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, Tallinn
SalaryExpert — Software Engineer salary, Tallinn (2026)

visa-access.summary: A modern, digital application process with modest funds bars, but Estonia has no mission in Bangladesh, so applications route via a third country, and there is no student fast-track.

pros.text: Estonia's e-state makes the study residence-permit process unusually streamlined, with modest proof-of-funds requirements.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban or heightened scrutiny.

cons.text: Estonia has no embassy in Bangladesh, so the long-stay visa/residence permit must be lodged/decided via a third-country mission (e.g. New Delhi), adding friction.
There is no student fast-track scheme.

links.title: Study in Estonia — visa & residence permit (official portal)

citizenship.summary: The decisive failure for this profile: Estonia does not allow dual citizenship for naturalised citizens, so a naturalising Bangladeshi must renounce BD nationality, and the path needs 8 years plus a B1 Estonian exam.

pros.text: The Estonian passport is strong (Henley 2026: ~183 visa-free destinations, top-tier EU mobility).

cons.text: Estonia does not permit dual citizenship for naturalised citizens — a naturalising Bangladeshi must renounce BD nationality, failing the couple's core retain-BD goal.
Naturalisation requires 8 years' residence (5 on a permanent permit), a B1 Estonian-language exam (a hard Finno-Ugric language), a Constitution/Citizenship-Act exam and an oath.

links.title: Police and Border Guard Board — applying for Estonian citizenship (official)
Estonian nationality law (overview)

post-study-work.summary: Graduates get a 9-month (270-day) job-search extension with no employer needed, and recent graduates are exempt from the foreign-worker salary threshold — a short but practical runway.

pros.text: On graduation the study permit can be extended 270 days (~9 months) to find work, with no employer required and the right to live and work during the search.
Recent graduates are exempt from the standard salary threshold and the Unemployment Insurance Fund permission, easing the move to a work permit; each spouse qualifies.

cons.text: The 9-month window is short next to Canada's 3-year PGWP or Germany's 18 months.

links.title: Study in Estonia — staying after studies (official portal)

spouse-family.summary: Family migration and free schooling exist, and if both spouses study each holds an independent track, though student-phase dependent work is conditional.

pros.text: Family members can join, children attend public school free, and if both spouses study each holds their own permit and 9-month job-search route.

cons.text: The dependent spouse's work access during the study phase is conditional, and family migration requires proving means.

links.title: Study in Estonia — bringing family (official portal)

msc-study.summary: Affordable English-taught CS/AI at TalTech and Tartu (~€3k–6k/yr) with generous student work rights, though Tartu is ending non-EU tuition waivers from 2026/27.

pros.text: TalTech and the University of Tartu offer English-taught CS/AI master's at low non-EU tuition (~€3,000–6,000/yr), and students may work freely provided it doesn't hinder studies.
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible, and a master's qualifies for the 9-month job-search permit.

cons.text: The English-taught catalogue is small, and the University of Tartu is ending non-EU tuition waivers from 2026/27.

links.title: TalTech — Tuition fee rates (official)
University of Tartu — Cost of tuition (official)

pr-pathway.summary: Long-term residence is reachable after 5 years, a clear route, but it carries a B1 Estonian-language requirement.

pros.text: Long-term (permanent) residence is reachable after 5 years of continuous residence, with study and work time building the route.

cons.text: Permanent residence requires a B1 Estonian-language exam, a meaningful burden for English-first applicants.

links.title: Police and Border Guard Board — long-term residence (official)

income-cost.summary: A low ~20–22% flat tax and cheap living make decent Tallinn salaries go far, with affordable housing relative to Western Europe.

pros.text: A flat ~20–22% income tax plus low living costs let two tech salaries net a solid surplus, with Tallinn housing affordable by Western-European standards.

cons.text: Absolute salaries are lower than in the larger Western markets, so savings/remittance potential is good rather than exceptional.

links.title: Study in Estonia — cost of living (official portal)

healthcare.summary: Good e-health-enabled public care once employed (Estonian Health Insurance Fund), but non-EU students rely on private insurance during study.

pros.text: Employment triggers public health insurance (Estonian Health Insurance Fund) with strong e-health infrastructure; family members can be co-insured.

cons.text: Non-EU students are not automatically in the public system and must hold private insurance during study; the welfare net is smaller than in the Nordics.

links.title: Study in Estonia — health insurance (official portal)

culture-language.summary: English works well in Tallinn's digital/startup world and the young are highly proficient, but Estonian (a hard Finno-Ugric language) is needed for daily life and status, in a small, homogeneous society.

pros.text: English works well in Tallinn's startup/digital sphere and among younger Estonians, and livability (nature, digital convenience) is good.

cons.text: Estonian — a difficult Finno-Ugric language — is required for daily life and for status (B1 for PR and citizenship).
Estonia is small and ethnically homogeneous with little exposure to South-Asian Muslim newcomers.

links.title: Study in Estonia — living in Estonia (official portal)

safety-law.summary: Safe and with upper-tier EU rule of law, though the very small Muslim presence means little track record of accommodating visible minorities.

pros.text: Low crime, upper-tier EU rule of law and reliable, low-corruption institutions; women's safety is good.

cons.text: With a very small Muslim/South-Asian population, there is little institutional track record of accommodating visible minorities, and some xenophobia exists.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: Stable, liberal and digitally minded toward skilled migration, with the far-right EKRE present but out of government; immigration is a low-salience issue given small numbers.

pros.text: Institutions are stable and the e-Residency-minded state is relatively open to skilled and digital workers.

cons.text: The far-right EKRE is a significant opposition force, and an immigration quota limits non-EU economic-migration numbers.

links.title: Study in Estonia — official portal

tax.summary: A simple, low flat income tax (~20–22%) with a famously easy digital system, offset by no expat regime and no confirmed Bangladesh tax treaty.

pros.text: A flat ~20–22% income tax with a world-leading digital filing system; tax is individual with no second-earner penalty.

cons.text: There is no special expat tax regime, and no Estonia–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty is confirmed (BD has DTAs with only ~36 countries).

links.title: Study in Estonia — taxes & working (official portal)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Among the weakest dimensions: Estonia's Muslim community is tiny (~0.7%, mostly Tatar/Azeri), there is no purpose-built mosque, halal is limited, and there is essentially no Bangladeshi community.

pros.text: The Estonian Islamic Centre in Tallinn provides a worship space, and Tallinn's small Muslim community is generally well-integrated and low-conflict.

cons.text: Muslims are only ~0.7% (~10,000, mostly Tatar/Azeri) with no purpose-built mosque and limited halal, and essentially no established Bangladeshi community or South-Asian ecosystem.

links.title: Islam in Estonia (overview)
Estonian Islamic Centre (overview)
